title: bizarre strangulation motif
wikitext:
[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Sun Apr 24, 2011 2:25 pm Let's talk about the bizarre strangulation motif running through the latter part of the series! Why strangulation? Why not poking, or judo-chopping, or flipping people the bird?

[]:             Quote Post by katastroff » Sun Apr 24, 2011 3:51 pm gatotsu911 wrote: Let's talk about the bizarre strangulation motif running through the latter part of the series! Why strangulation? Why not poking, or judo-chopping, or flipping people the bird?

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Sun Apr 24, 2011 6:34 pm Because the three examples you used would've been hilarious.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Sun Apr 24, 2011 7:08 pm Don't forget the "choking" scene at the beginning of EOE...

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Sun Apr 24, 2011 7:17 pm My goodness, it seems I've been threadified!! @Warren Peace: Any time I refer to "the series" I am including the original movies unless otherwise noted. @Katastroff: Good answer!! You get an A. In addition, I would posit that strangulation is simply the most impulsive act of violence, despair and hatred that there is. A little bit of soul-spilling, if I may: I did not fully comprehend the significance of the act of strangulation within the context of the plot, specifically the final scene, for a while after I'd seen it. I eventually reached a certain point, however, where I was at my absolute lowest, and felt more anger and despair and helplessness than I've ever experienced in my life, and when I reached that point the only thing I could think of doing was strangling someone until they stopped moving. I didn't do it, fortunately, but from that point onward I felt that I had gained a greater understanding of this particular act of violence, the feelings associated with it, and its use as a motif within the show. I wonder whether Anno didn't have a similar experience. Whew! That was heavy, man. Let's lighten things up a little. I was going to add "bitch slapping" to my list of strangulation-alternatives, but then I realized that people do in fact get bitch-slapped multiple times in Evangelion , and if I were hopelessly anal-retentive and/or insane (but not the good kind of insane, like you want) I could try and read some meaning into the "Bitch Slap Motif". In fact, if any mods want to threadify that, they are cordially invited to do so.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sun Apr 24, 2011 10:36 pm Strangulation is something very raw as one can do it really quickly out of an impulse (beating someone to death, for example, requires multiple steps - subdue the person, pin them down, gradually injure them...) and can also be done with simply your hands at any time without a weapon as proxy. It is therefore one of the "rawest" killing methods.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Apr 24, 2011 10:50 pm gatotsu911 wrote: Let's talk about the bizarre strangulation motif running through the latter part of the series! Why strangulation? Why not poking, or judo-chopping, or flipping people the bird?

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sun Apr 24, 2011 11:55 pm Sachi wrote: What's so bizarre about it? As you sort of implied before, it has a lot to do with the hands motif. Not only are hands a symbol of confidence, determination, and responsibility, but they also symbolize power, specifically the power to create and destroy. We see this also during the sandbox scene (even though he didn't literally destroy the pyramid with his hands, it's still implied figuratively). In the context of strangulation, we see it being used as a symbol of death (AKA: destruction) on a raw emotional level. Shinji holds life and death in his hands, and he decides to use it to cause death in the instance of strangling Asuka. How many instances of strangulation are there in the series? I'm sure each have varying meanings behind them. Here are the ones I can pull from the top of my head: - Unit-03 against Unit-01 - Unit-01 against Unit-03 - Shinji against Asuka (pre-Instrumentality) - Shinji against Asuka (One More Final: I Need You)

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Mon Apr 25, 2011 1:16 am gatotsu911 wrote: @Warren Peace: Any time I refer to "the series" I am including the original movies unless otherwise noted.

[]:             Quote Post by svenge » Mon Apr 25, 2011 1:28 am Warren Peace wrote: Er, what? EOE was an original series movie.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Apr 25, 2011 2:26 am I think others have covered the basics. Hands are an important motif in NGE as it is, being metaphors for life, death, choice, passivity and determination. All of the "life" motifs are associated with open palms throughout the series, so I think it works well that "death" is associated with closed fists. There's certainly something very primal about strangulation, as it's one constant act in which the life slowly drains out of the life-form being strangled.

[]:             Quote Post by flapjack » Mon Apr 25, 2011 4:02 am bump

[]:             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Mon Apr 25, 2011 4:46 am Strangulation is essentially the most intimate form of murder - a twisted form of cradling, with two people being as physically close as it gets.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Mon Apr 25, 2011 8:30 am flapjack wrote: Asuka, on the other hand , likes to skillfully kick things in 2.0.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Tue Apr 26, 2011 10:18 am There's one aspect of the whole strangulation "thing" that I never understood -- in EOE, why does Shinji break out his possessed Unit-03 impression when he strangles Asuka (in his fantasy)? He has the same slumped-over posture as the infected Eva. Then, the roles are reversed, with Asuka resembling Unit-03 after its neck is broken. It's a call-back to that scene, but I don't know why.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Apr 26, 2011 11:17 am Warren Peace wrote: There's one aspect of the whole strangulation "thing" that I never understood -- in EOE, why does Shinji break out his possessed Unit-03 impression when he strangles Asuka (in his fantasy)? He has the same slumped-over posture as the infected Eva. Then, the roles are reversed, with Asuka resembling Unit-03 after its neck is broken. It's a call-back to that scene, but I don't know why.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Tue Apr 26, 2011 11:34 am No, it seems too particular to be a coincidence. Especially when he lifts her right off the ground, arms dangling (like he broke her neck). I think it has to be intentional. My best guess is that it represents Shinji's complete reversal of attitude... in the Bardiel scene, he's not willing to kill anybody. In the later moment, he wants to kill everybody.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Apr 26, 2011 11:40 am Warren Peace wrote: No, it seems too particular to be a coincidence. Especially when he lifts her right off the ground, arms dangling (like he broke her neck). I think it has to be intentional. My best guess is that it represents Shinji's complete reversal of attitude... in the Bardiel scene, he's not willing to kill anybody. In the later moment, he wants to kill everybody.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Tue Apr 26, 2011 12:16 pm Yeah, but the fact that her neck is broken at all seems like a tip off. Why give Shinji such super-human strength? Why have him lift her off the ground? That the exact same thing happens to Unit-03 seems beyond coincidental to me.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Tue Apr 26, 2011 12:20 pm Warren Peace wrote: My best guess is that it represents Shinji's complete reversal of attitude... in the Bardiel scene, he's not willing to kill anybody. In the later moment, he wants to kill everybody.
